verificationNote: Requirements constructed from IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, and NACSA Act 854 obligations. Not endorsed by any standards body — verify against authoritative sources before use in formal assessments.
name: Incident Detection and Response
description: Requirements for detecting, classifying, responding to, and reporting OT security incidents. Includes NACSA Act 854 notification obligations for NCII-designated entities. The 6-hour notification requirement under s26 creates a hard operational deadline that requires pre-existing detection and classification capability.

nacsaNotificationSummary.trigger: Any cyber security incident that has or may have a significant impact on designated NCII systems
nacsaNotificationSummary.significantImpact: Disruption to NCII services exceeding thresholds defined in P.U.(A) 220/2024 (Notification Regulations) — for most sectors, this means service disruption exceeding 4 hours or affecting a defined number of customers

timeline.initialNotification: 6 hours from becoming aware of significant-impact incident
timeline.followUp: Every 72 hours until NACSA confirms case closure
timeline.recordKeeping: 7 years for all incidents (significant and non-significant)

nacsaNotificationSummary.basis: NACSA Act 854 s26; Cyber Security (Notification of Cyber Security Incident) Regulations 2024 (P.U.(A) 220/2024)

requirements.name: OT security monitoring platform
requirements.description: Deploy a passive OT network monitoring platform that provides continuous visibility into OT network traffic, asset communication patterns, and security events without disrupting real-time control functions.

legal.summary: Continuous monitoring is required at SL 3. Without OT monitoring, detecting incidents within the 6-hour window required by NACSA s26 is infeasible for network-based attacks.

technical.summary: Deploy passive OT monitoring sensors on core OT network segments. Platform provides: asset inventory, protocol analysis, anomaly detection, threat detection with OT-specific signatures.
actions: Deploy passive sensors on SPAN/TAP at: DCS zone, HMI zone, IDMZ, engineering zone boundaries
Configure asset baseline — expected devices, protocols, communication patterns
Integrate with SIEM/SOC for alert triage and correlation
Configure OT-specific detection rules (unusual Modbus writes, new engineering connections, protocol anomalies)
Tune detection to OT operational patterns to reduce false positives

governance.summary: OT monitoring platform alerts reviewed by OT security team or managed SOC with OT expertise. Alert review process defined with escalation paths.
actions: Alert triage procedure defined with SLA
Escalation path from analyst to OT security engineer to management
Monthly detection effectiveness review

slMapping.sl2: Centralised log collection with automated alerting for defined events
slMapping.sl3: Passive OT monitoring platform with 24/7 alert coverage and OT SOC
slMapping.sl4: Dedicated OT security monitoring centre; real-time safety system anomaly detection

requirements.name: OT incident classification and NACSA notification decision
requirements.description: A documented procedure must exist for classifying OT security events and determining whether they meet the NACSA s26 significant-impact threshold — within the 6-hour notification window.

legal.summary: s26(1) requires notification within 6 hours of becoming aware of a significant-impact incident. 'Becoming aware' includes reasonable grounds to suspect — not confirmed attribution. The decision to notify must be made quickly, under uncertainty, without waiting for full investigation.
legal.owner: OT Security Lead / Incident Response Lead

technical.summary: Pre-defined incident classification matrix mapping alert types to significance determinations. Decision tree for NACSA notification with designated decision-maker.
actions: Develop incident classification matrix (alert type → significance determination)
Define designated person(s) with authority to make NACSA notification decision
Pre-populate NACSA notification template for rapid completion under pressure
Test classification procedure in tabletop exercises using realistic OT alert scenarios
Maintain NACSA contact details and submission process in incident runbook

governance.summary: Notification decision is a time-critical management decision requiring clear authority and pre-tested procedure. Notification is mandatory — failure to notify is a criminal offence.
actions: Clear authority for notification decision defined in incident response policy
NACSA contact and submission process documented and tested
Notification decision documented with rationale (notified or not notified, and why)
Legal/compliance review of classification matrix

evidenceItems: Tabletop exercise records including classification decisions

slMapping.sl2: Documented incident classification with NACSA notification procedure
slMapping.sl3: Tested notification procedure with tabletop evidence; sub-6h notification SLA demonstrated in exercises
slMapping.sl4: Automated alert triage support; duty officer system for 24/7 notification capability

requirements.name: OT incident response plan
requirements.description: A documented OT-specific incident response plan addressing the unique characteristics of OT incidents: physical process impact, 24/7 operational continuity, limited forensic tools, vendor dependency, and regulatory notification requirements.

legal.summary: IEC 62443-2-1 requires an OT security incident response programme. NACSA s18 requires security measures proportionate to risk — incident response is a core risk mitigation measure.

technical.summary: OT IRP includes: detection, containment (without causing process upset), evidence collection, recovery, NACSA notification. Separate runbooks per OT system type.
actions: Develop OT-specific IRP separate from (or as appendix to) IT IRP
Include runbooks for: malware on OT workstation, unauthorised PLC change, OT network anomaly, ransomware in OT environment, vendor remote access incident
Define containment options that do not cause process upsets (e.g., isolate HMI zone without disconnecting PLC from field)
Document evidence collection for OT (historian data, network captures from IDMZ, PLC program export)
Annual IRP test (tabletop minimum; full exercise preferred)

governance.summary: OT IRP owned by OT operations manager with OT security engineer as technical lead. Annual review and post-incident update.
actions: Annual IRP review and update
Post-incident review updates IRP within 30 days
Executive briefing on IRP scope and capability annually

evidenceItems: IRP training records for OT staff

slMapping.sl3: OT IRP with system-specific runbooks; annual tabletop exercise; dedicated OT IR capability
slMapping.sl4: Full tested IRP; 24/7 response capability; dedicated OT forensics toolkit

requirements.name: Forensic evidence preservation in OT
requirements.description: Preserve forensic evidence during OT incidents without disrupting process operations or causing loss of evidence due to system restarts, overwriting of logs, or loss of volatile data.

basis: NACSA Act 854 s26 (30-day final report requires investigation findings)
legal.summary: The 30-day final incident report required by NACSA s26 requires documented investigation findings. Forensic evidence from the OT environment (historian records, network captures, log files) supports this report and may be required for regulatory review.

technical.summary: Pre-position forensic collection capability: network capture at IDMZ, historian data export procedure, log backup procedure. Establish chain of custody for OT evidence.
actions: Pre-configure network capture capability at IDMZ (wireshark/tcpdump on monitoring system)
Document historian data export procedure (which tags, which timeframe, export format)
Identify all log sources per OT component with location and export method
Establish chain of custody procedure for OT forensic evidence
Maintain OT forensic toolkit (laptop with OT protocol analysers: Wireshark with OT dissectors, pymodbus, DNP3 analyser)

governance.summary: Evidence preservation decision must be made within the first hour of incident recognition — before restarts or log overwriting occur.
actions: Evidence preservation is step 1 in all OT incident runbooks
Evidence integrity maintained through chain of custody documentation
Legal hold notification to relevant personnel when NACSA investigation is anticipated

evidenceItems: Evidence preservation checklist per system type

slMapping.sl2: Log backup procedure; historian data export documented
slMapping.sl3: Pre-configured forensic collection; chain of custody procedure
slMapping.sl4: Dedicated OT forensics capability; immutable evidence storage; expert OT forensic analyst
